text: – they are simply a matter of taste, and about taste we cannot argue (see here Szakolczai’s discussion, 2013c: 34–5). At the level of political philosophy, it was this very position that was argued for so famously by John Rawls and applied to political liberalism: we cannot know about what people aim for, all we can do is to generate a just system of distribution, an abstract system of fairness and justice without any reference to values, a system that can be brought to function no matter which human beings are inserted into it (see Thomassen 2012e). Here we must start from a well-known, if often overlooked fact: that the notion of ‘interests’ stems from the two Latin words, ‘inter’ and ‘esse’. The term literally refers to that which lies between, or happens between, beings or being: it is a ‘being in between’ (Horvath 2013: 195; Wydra 2009). The notion of ‘self-interest’ is literally meaningless. This is because interests are never simply ‘given’, nor are they simply deducible by ‘inserting’ an abstractly modelled individual into a social situation with all the relevant variables under control. Following Girard, what we want, what we desire, happens between human beings. The value of an object, lies not in its intrinsic qualities, but in how that object is viewed by more than one subject. With respect to economic value, of for example stocks, such value emerges in a temporal in-betweenness, in the tension between present and future; and a decisive factor here involves the expectations that human beings (investors in particular) have, their projections of the future, their projections of other people’s projections, matched against other people’s projections of your projections – and so on, into the myriad of economic models that have tried to map this essentially very social, mimetic and unpredictable situation of in-betweenness of subjects in the in-betweenness of now and tomorrow. In fact, as Plato and later Girard argued, human desires are utterly and foundationally mimetic. It was this perspective that Gabriel Tarde took up so convincingly. Shortly before his death in 1904, Tarde published La Psychologie Economique (1902/3), and developed a perspective recently summed up by Latour and Lepinay (2009). For Tarde, the ‘interests’ that are the very subject matter of the modern economy are indeed very ‘passionate’. ‘Far from being objective, interests involve a state of passion, a tension and intention, not to be sought at the level of the hidden essence of a human being’ (Horvath 2013: 196). The question then becomes how such passions are mediated or consciously manipulated. As further discussed by Horvath, this was the question that Albert Hirschman (1997) famously took up in The Passions and the Interests. Hirschman argued that passions were considered subject to control by precisely that idiom of objectifiable ‘interests’ that modern economics takes as its point of departure. This did not happen in the abstract, or in single minds and their calculations: it happened as a By Way of Conclusion: Out of Liminality 221 historical process, as part of the transition to modernity. Part I of Hirschman’s book was fittingly called How the Interests were called upon to Counteract the Passions. But why had passions become such a problem that they had to be ‘counteracted’? The answer given by Hirschman was simple: this was the historical situation of incessant religious wars, the liminal period of the seventeenth century discussed in Chapter 5. The behavourial foundations of capitalism cannot be disconnected from economic history, and economic history – the origins and foundations of capitalism – cannot be separated from political history. And underneath it all rests an anthropological dimension, what Weber called the level of Menschentum, the kind of human being through which the institutional levels operate. The question posed by Hirschman is quite Weberian in style: how is it that an activity formerly judged amoral would, at a given point in time, be considered morally right? When St Augustine listed the three most basic sins of ‘fallen man’, lust for money and possessions was one of them. Machiavelli and Hobbes would later declare that religious values could no longer tie individuals to righteousness; they could not tame the disorderly passions of men. Hirschman’s argument is that ‘interests’ became the tamers of passions. From the eighteenth century the concept of interest came to be generally accepted, and was also ethically ‘ennobled’. It was an important part of this ‘acceptance’ that the concept remained flexibly and loosely defined, but according to Hirschman that did not prevent it from performing two essential functions: predictability and constancy. Those functions were building blocks for both modern politics and modern economics. The love of money-making was ‘softened’ and normalized, accepted and then venerated. In the theory of Adam Smith, the pursuit of self-interest was eventually identified as the very key to prosperity. The development was equally political. In standard approaches to politics, democracy was to be conceived as a public arena where actors could argue and fight out their interests, and rationally so. In the writings of thinkers like Montesquieu, Sir James Stuart and John Millar (all discussed by Hirschman in Chapter 2 of The Passions and the Interests), the idea was developed that the by- products of interest (i.e. commerce and industry) would also serve to constrain authoritarian decision-making by the sovereign (Locatelli 2006), and therefore pave the way for political freedoms – a cornerstone of liberalism ever since. As summed up by Horvath (2013: 196), ‘the objectivation of ‘passionate interests’ into a rational and calculable, manageable and predictable form was considered as a solution to the religious and political crisis that otherwise proved intractable’. The essential problem is that the ‘stability’ involved here is not generated from within experienced life, but comes to function as an externally imposed ideology of the system. The system is cut off from human experience, even as it claims to be based on it. In fact, the entire world-view is inherently problematic because it fails to treat the anthropological dimension in any serious way. Following Tarde, any
